title: neophloga pervillei
intro: Neophloga pervillei, commonly known as the 'wild marigold', is a flowering plant species native to the island of Réunion in the Indian Ocean. Research suggests that this plant has several health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help in reducing the risk of chronic diseases such as cancer and cardiovascular disease. The therapeutic actions of Neophloga pervillei include antimicrobial, antiviral, and antifungal activities, making it a potential remedy for various infections and diseases. The bioactive constituents responsible for its therapeutic properties include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which have been identified in various studies on the plant's chemical composition.
parts: Neophloga pervillei has many medicinal parts, such as the roots, which contain flavonoids and phenolic acids, and are used to treat various ailments including fever and infections. The leaves of the plant contain essential oils and triterpenoids, which exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The flowers of Neophloga pervillei are rich in sesquiterpenes and flavonoids, and are used to treat respiratory issues such as bronchitis and asthma. The plant's stems and seeds also contain medicinal constituents, including alkaloids and glycosides, which have been shown to have antimicrobial and antipyretic properties.
side_effects: Neophloga pervillei can have side effects if used improperly, such as gastrointestinal issues, including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, due to its high levels of toxic compounds. Prolonged exposure to its extracts can also cause renal failure, as well as cardiovascular problems like hypertension and arrhythmia. In severe cases, improper use of the plant may lead to neurological issues, including seizures and coma. Additionally, Neophloga pervillei can cause allergic reactions, such as skin irritation and anaphylaxis, in sensitive individuals.
